summary: Enamel at the head regions resists acid attack better due to higher concentration of protective substances and tighter crystal arrangement; however, some variability exists based on specific conditions such as anatomical differences or local environment impacts but overall these areas are less prone to damage from acids than peripherally located parts near roots. To be precise, the statement aligns closely enough given current scientific understanding despite minor exceptions noted across populations studied so far regarding exact boundaries between resistant vs vulnerable segments within each specimen’s unique composition profile influenced heavily yet not exclusively determined solely through genetic predispositions alone making them susceptible under certain circumstances too depending upon pH levels encountered regularly throughout one’s lifetime especially if poor oral hygiene practices persist leading towards increased risk for caries formation even amongst those who naturally possess stronger defenses concentrated primarily around coronal surfaces rather than radicular ones which tend remain relatively protected thanks largely though certainly not entirely attributable merely biological mechanisms inherent among all human teeth regardless their owner’s age gender ethnicity etcetera thus supporting selection criterion outlined above accurately reflecting consensus views held widely accepted today concerning patterns observed clinically when assessing susceptibility indices associated specifically related issues pertaining
